name: suite_layout
lever_classification: methodology — which participant environment to create
consequences: Determines participant comfort and naturalistic behavior, potentially affecting data quality.
options: Compact shared bedrooms with enhanced video monitoring
Private suites with reduced camera presence
Suite clusters with a mix of private and shared spaces

name: sensing_model
consequences: Impacts data resolution, false alarm rates, and annotation throughput.
options: Tiered acquisition model with low-burden monitoring and enhanced nights
Full PSG on all participant nights
Event-driven deployment of full PSG for flagged events

name: recruitment_channels
consequences: Affects participant diversity, yield rates, and study efficiency.
options: University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and regional neurologist referrals
Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network and online advertising
Direct outreach to local sleep disorder support groups

name: participant_compensation
consequences: Influences participant engagement, retention, and yield rates.
options: €80 per night plus meals
€100 per night plus meals
€120 per night with additional incentives for longer stays

name: event-triage_tool_development
consequences: Impacts manual review burden, data quality, and study efficiency.
options: Development of semi-automated tools with dual human scorer agreement
Use of machine learning algorithms for event prioritization
Manual review only with no automated triage

name: participant_screening_process
consequences: Affects participant selection, yield rates, and study validity.
options: Structured pre-admission pathway with clinical interview and collateral history
Inclusion of additional screening tools for sleep disorders
Simplified participant selection process with reduced screening

name: data_sharing_protocol
consequences: Impacts data accessibility, reuse, and study impact.
options: De-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements
Public deposition of annotated EEG-BIDS-compatible format data
Data sharing restricted to study collaborators only

name: suite_expansion_strategy
lever_classification: operations — how to expand the facility's physical capacity
consequences: Deciding on how to expand the facility's physical capacity will directly impact staffing ratios, data quality, and participant recruitment. If expansion is too aggressive, it may compromise the pilot study's integrity.
options: Expand the existing property by converting adjacent residential units for additional sleep suites
Acquire a new nearby property to consolidate research activities under one roof
Implement a phased expansion plan, renovating and equipping suites in stages
review: The options all assume that expanding capacity will directly correlate with increased participant recruitment, but the relationship between suite numbers and admissions is not linear. A deeper analysis of historical data or a controlled experiment would be needed to accurately model this relationship.

name: technician_training_program
lever_classification: execution — how to train research technicians
consequences: Deciding on how to train research technicians will directly impact data quality, participant safety, and staff burnout. If training is inadequate, it may lead to errors in data annotation or participant care.
options: Develop a comprehensive, standardized training program with regular assessments and certifications
Implement an apprenticeship model where senior technicians mentor junior staff
Provide on-the-job training with minimal formal instruction
review: The options all assume that technician training is a one-time event, but research has shown that continuous learning and skill-building are essential in high-stakes environments like this facility.

name: participant_retention_tactics
lever_classification: governance — how to retain participants in the study
consequences: Deciding on how to retain participants will directly impact data quality, participant satisfaction, and study completion rates. If retention strategies are inadequate, it may lead to high dropout rates or biased data.
options: Implement a personalized engagement program with tailored incentives for each participant
Develop a comprehensive survey and feedback system to monitor participant satisfaction
Offer flexible scheduling and compensation packages to accommodate participants' needs
review: The options all assume that participant retention is solely dependent on extrinsic factors, but research has shown that intrinsic motivators like sense of purpose or personal growth can be just as effective.

name: data_sharing_policies
lever_classification: dissemination — how to share data from the study with other researchers or organizations
consequences: Deciding on data sharing policies will directly impact participant privacy, research collaboration, and scientific reproducibility. If policies are too restrictive, it may limit opportunities for cross-disciplinary collaboration or data reuse.
options: Implement a strict, opt-in data-sharing policy with minimal metadata release
Develop a tiered data-sharing model with varying levels of access based on research proposals
Adopt an open-data policy with unrestricted sharing and reuse
review: The options all assume that data sharing is solely dependent on technical infrastructure, but research has shown that social and cultural contexts play a significant role in shaping data-sharing practices.

name: research_collaboration_model
lever_classification: governance — how to collaborate with other researchers or organizations in the study
consequences: Deciding on a research collaboration model will directly impact knowledge sharing, resource allocation, and scientific impact. If collaborations are too loose, it may lead to duplicated efforts or missed opportunities.
options: Develop a formal partnership with the University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology
Establish a collaborative network with external research institutions and organizations
Implement a hub-and-spoke model with centralized data management and decentralized analysis
review: The options all assume that research collaborations are solely dependent on institutional relationships, but research has shown that informal networks and personal connections can be just as effective.

name: participant_compensation_model
lever_classification: product — how to compensate participants for their participation in the study
consequences: Deciding on a participant compensation model will directly impact recruitment rates, participant satisfaction, and data quality. If compensation is inadequate, it may lead to biased or incomplete data.
options: Offer a flat rate of €80 per night with no additional incentives
Implement a tiered compensation system based on participant engagement and contributions
Develop a personalized compensation package tailored to each participant's needs
review: The options all assume that participant compensation is solely dependent on extrinsic factors, but research has shown that intrinsic motivators like sense of purpose or personal growth can be just as effective.

name: suite_configuration
lever_classification: operations — how to configure the residential research unit's living spaces for optimal participant experience and data quality
consequences: Configuring suites for optimal participant comfort and safety can lead to higher participant retention rates, but may compromise data quality if participants become too comfortable.
options: Configure suites with adjustable lighting and temperature controls
Use soundproofing materials to minimize external noise
Install smart home automation systems for enhanced comfort

name: participant_screening_processes
lever_classification: governance — what criteria to use for participant selection and exclusion
consequences: Implementing rigorous screening processes can reduce the risk of including participants with uncontrolled medical conditions, but may lead to delays in participant recruitment.
options: Use a comprehensive clinical interview and collateral history from bed partners or household members
Require prior polysomnography results for all participants
Conduct on-site neurological screenings for suspected epilepsy mimics

name: technician_training_programs
lever_classification: operations — how to train research staff in data collection and annotation
consequences: Investing in comprehensive technician training can enhance data quality and reduce annotation burdens, but may divert resources away from other critical areas.
options: Develop a multi-day training program for new technicians
Provide ongoing workshops and peer mentoring for experienced technicians
Offer specialized training for technicians working with sensitive patient populations

name: recruitment_channel_mixtures
lever_classification: operations — how to recruit participants for the residential research unit
consequences: Diversifying recruitment channels can increase participant enrollment rates, but may lead to higher costs and logistical complexities.
options: Focus on a single primary recruitment channel (e.g., University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic)
Use a mix of clinical referrals, online advertising, and community outreach programs
Leverage social media platforms and online forums for participant recruitment

name: participant_compensation_models
lever_classification: governance — what policies to implement for participant compensation
consequences: Implementing generous participant compensation models can enhance participant retention rates, but may compromise data quality if participants become too focused on financial rewards.
options: Offer a standard per-night compensation of €80 plus meals
Provide additional incentives for participants who complete extended stays or produce high-quality data
Use a tiered compensation model that rewards participants for achieving specific research milestones

name: data_sharing_pact_negotiations
lever_classification: governance — what policies to implement for sharing participant data with external partners
consequences: Negotiating data sharing agreements with external partners can facilitate collaboration and advance scientific knowledge, but may compromise participant privacy.
options: Require all external partners to sign a comprehensive data use agreement
Establish a tiered access model that restricts sensitive data to authorized researchers only
Use a 'data-sharing-by-default' approach that prioritizes openness and transparency
